# What the Architect Owns {#sec-what-architect-owns}

```{=latex}
\abstract*{Architecture 2.0 changes how architecture work is divided, but it does not remove responsibility for architectural decisions. AI systems, tools, specialists, and automated checks may produce artifacts, run experiments, reject candidates, and prepare recommendations. A named architect or authorized architecture role still sets the problem boundary, assigns authority, decides what evidence is sufficient, and owns consequential decisions. This chapter returns to the opening lighthouse request to show what that responsibility requires. The work may be distributed among named people or organizational roles, but responsibility cannot disappear.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
As AI systems take on more architecture work, what must the architect still
understand, decide, and remain answerable for?
:::

@sec-loop-patterns-across-stack showed that permitted AI work, evidence,
rejection checks, rollback, and decision rights must change with the operating
regime. Someone still has to set and revise those limits. This final chapter
asks what remains with the architect as more of the work passes to AI systems,
tools, and specialists.

The lighthouse prompt introduced in @sec-moonshot makes the question concrete.
Its short request names a workload class, ISA family, power target, process,
and deliverable, but it does not specify workload coverage, software and ISA
contracts, meaningful alternatives, the tool path, physical evidence, rejected
candidates, or the owner of the resulting decision. Those choices turn the
request into architecture work. Reading the prompt only as a request for a
future hardware foundation model misses that work[^fn-arch-hardware-foundation-model].

[^fn-arch-hardware-foundation-model]: The phrase names a tempting but incomplete reading of the lighthouse prompt, a single broadly trained hardware model that maps intent to designs. Such a model would still need an architecture study grounded in representations, tools, experiments, evidence, challenge, and human judgment. When that work iterates, a design loop organizes its execution and analysis.

Architects already set scope, interpret tool evidence, and own decisions. AI
does not invent those responsibilities. It can increase the amount of delegated
artifact production, tool use, and proposal generation, which makes authority,
review capacity, and traceability harder to leave implicit. Cards, replay
packages, and checks can help another architect inspect the work, but the
architectural finding or decision remains the point.

## The Architect Owns the Decision

Not every check has to pass through one person's hands. Simulators, policy
checks, and regression suites may reject candidates or advance routine
transitions automatically. The architect or another authorized role defines
the scope of that automation, what each check may block or advance, the
evidence needed for each class of decision, the escalation and waiver paths,
and the point at which a consequential decision requires recorded acceptance.
Execution can be distributed, but responsibility for the decision must remain
assigned.

Here, *the architect* means the authorized role answerable for the decision,
not one individual performing every operation. Large programs may assign workload scope, software
compatibility, physical signoff, security, and product acceptance to different
authorized roles. The relevant person or organization must be named for each
decision, and its rights, handoffs, and limits must remain explicit as work
crosses models, tools, and abstraction levels.

That distinction is especially sharp near tapeout authorization. A generative
model or automated optimizer may produce RTL, analyze reports, or recommend a
candidate, but it is not answerable for a tapeout decision. Some software
systems are also hard to roll back, while tapeout is unusually difficult to
reverse. A bug committed to silicon can require a respin and substantial
schedule delay against a design investment that industry estimates put at
roughly \$725M at 2\ nm [@Shilov2023IBSDesignCost].
The 2024 Wilson Research Group study reports first-silicon success at 14
percent among its respondents [@Foster2025WilsonVerification2024]. That is a
high-consequence outcome measure, not a project-specific respin probability or
a diagnosis of why a first spin failed. A named tapeout or product role
therefore has to own the acceptance criteria, unresolved waivers, and tapeout
decision. AI may allow more artifacts to be delegated and send more candidates
to review. It does not remove the need for an architect to understand the
cross-layer consequences, decide whether the evidence supports advancement,
and remain answerable for the result.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter you can explain and assign the responsibilities that remain
with architects in AI-assisted architecture work. That means you can:

- **Distinguish** routine checks and transitions that may be automated from decision rights that must remain assigned and answerable;
- **Map** evidence to the consequence it can support without treating fidelity as authority;
- **Explain** why an architect must understand delegated abstractions, tools, and evidence without personally executing every step;
- **Answer** the strongest objections to Architecture 2.0 without treating automation as ownership;
- **Complete** an architecture study another architect can review, from a scoped question to a decision with a named owner;
- **Demonstrate** the six capabilities introduced in the Preface on a new architecture problem.
:::

## Return to the Moonshot

The lighthouse prompt makes the ownership question concrete:

> Design a low-power, 64-bit RISC-V-based compute subsystem for an
> XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workload. Realize it as a vector-capable
> CPU, accelerator, or SoC block under a 3\ W TDP target in a 3\ nm-class LP
> mobile process, and return a design-space report with evidence and rejected
> alternatives.

The prompt fixes part of the target, but it does not say which XRBench tasks and
deadlines establish workload coverage, which compiler and ISA behavior must be
preserved, or how the CPU, accelerator, and SoC alternatives should be
compared. It also leaves the tool sequence, physical evidence, rejected
candidates, and decision owner unspecified. An architect must resolve those
choices and define the checks that can reject a candidate before an AI-generated
artifact can support a recommendation.

::: {.callout-lighthouse title="The prompt is now a study brief"}
"XRBench-class" requires a versioned workload and a stated coverage claim.
"64-bit RISC-V-based" requires compatible compiler, simulator, and instruction
semantics. The CPU, accelerator, and SoC alternatives imply different
integration choices. The 3\ W and process targets require power, thermal, and
physical evidence appropriate to the proposed action. The report should retain
comparisons and rejected alternatives. The decision owner determines what is
fixed, what automation may change, which checks can stop the work, and who may
authorize the next step.
:::

Evidence fidelity does not determine authority. Proxies,
cycle-level simulation, RTL verification, emulation, synthesis,
place-and-route, and signoff checks differ in scope, cost, assumptions, and
independence. Their adequacy depends on the claim and proposed transition.
@tbl-evidence-by-consequence maps evidence to consequence, while
@tbl-decision-rights-matrix separates who may propose, execute, reject, approve
a waiver, recommend, and make the final decision. Stronger evidence does not
grant any of those permissions either.

When architects set evidence thresholds, they must also keep unlike cost
measures separate. Public sources illuminate several consequences of
high-consequence decisions, but they do not price the same event. A proxy sweep
reports compute time, a verification survey reports a share
of project schedule, node estimates describe whole-design investment, and an
Intel filing records one company's charge for a field defect. Plotting those
quantities on one dollar axis would imply a common denominator the sources do
not provide (@tbl-consequence-evidence).

| **Decision context** | **What the cited observation measures** | **What it does not measure** | **Decision implication** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Exploratory search | ArchGym reports that a proxy-backed sweep of roughly 4,000 experiments could be rerun in about two days [@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym]. | A dollar price for rerunning any architecture study | Search can be repeated cheaply only within the declared tool and workload boundary. |
| Experimental rework | The Wilson study reports verification taking a 50 to 60 percent median share of project schedule [@Foster2022WilsonVerificationStudy]. | The marginal time or cost of reversing one experimental decision | The architect should state which verification resource a new candidate consumes. |
| Implementation and integration | Public estimates range from roughly \$175M for a 10 nm design to more than \$540M at 5 nm; a secondary report gives an IBS estimate of \$725M for a large 2 nm chip developed from scratch without reusable IP [@BauerEtAl2020SemiconductorDesignManufacturing; @Shilov2023IBSDesignCost]. | A mask-set price, respin price, or amount lost when one decision changes | Late decisions can put accumulated program investment at risk, but the amount must be estimated for the actual project. |
| Field defect | Intel reported a \$475M pretax charge covering replacement and other costs associated with the Pentium floating-point divide problem [@Intel1995PentiumCharge]. | A universal recall or fleet-rollback cost | An irreversible action needs an owner and a reversal plan proportionate to its actual consequence. |

: **Consequence evidence has different denominators.** The cited observations
bound different questions and cannot be read as one quantitative cost ladder.
Their shared lesson is to name the exposed asset, reversal path, and evidence
required for the proposed action. {#tbl-consequence-evidence tbl-colwidths="[17,27,25,21]"}

The observations do not establish that every more consequential transition has a
predictable dollar multiplier. They establish why the people who may approve a
waiver or make the final decision must be named when actions become harder to
reverse. The mapping must name the actual consequence, not borrow a number from
a different project or event.

Evidence must also match the requested deliverable. A design-space report is
different from an RTL artifact. RTL functional verification is different from a
placed-and-routed implementation that has passed timing and physical signoff.
Logical correctness alone does not establish routability or satisfaction of
timing, power-integrity, and thermal constraints. Each step changes the evidence
standard. The same prompt can support a brainstorming study, a
research prototype, a simulator-backed design-space exploration, or a
high-consequence implementation study. Treating all of those deliverables as
the same is one of the fastest ways to overclaim.

An architect can use the design-loop card in @sec-architecture-20-ontology and
@sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card to turn the prompt into reviewable questions.
What task and comparison are being studied? Which state and actions are
represented? Which tools, methods, and feedback are available? What evidence
supports the claim, what would contradict it, and which alternatives failed?
Who may stop, waive, reopen, or approve the work?

The answers define which parts of the design space an automated method may
inspect or change. A representation that is too narrow can exclude useful
alternatives. One that grants broad actions without constraints and checks can
let the method optimize the wrong objective or cross a decision boundary.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="Check the Allowed Actions"}
Has a named role provided constraints, evidence checks, and stop conditions for
every setting the automated optimizer can change? If not, it may optimize an
invalid metric or cross an authority boundary unnoticed. If L2 cache size is a tunable action, an area-budget check
can reject oversize configurations, while a congestion check can screen
candidates likely to be unroutable.
:::

Enforcing those boundaries marks the shift from a prompt to a defined
architecture study. The prompt motivates the work. The architect must connect
the architecture question and alternatives to a tool path, evidence,
explanation, and decision.
A prompt-to-chip demonstration asks whether a system can produce an impressive
final artifact from a sentence. A more useful test asks whether another
architect can inspect enough of the work to understand what the artifact
establishes. When the work iterates, a design loop keeps the execution and
analysis available for that review.

## Decision Rights Must Remain Assigned

Writing a detailed study plan does not settle who may act on its results.
Models can propose, harnesses can execute, tools or reviewers can reject, policy
engines can route routine transitions, specialists can recommend, and an
authorized role can make the final decision. @tbl-decision-rights-matrix keeps
those assignments independent. Holding one right does not imply the others, and technical
capability does not confer authority on a model, tool, or specialist.

The rights may be distributed across authorized organizational roles. Named
roles remain answerable for framing the question, accepting a waiver, and
authorizing a consequential transition. A model can help prepare those
decisions; it cannot answer for the organizational outcome.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="Trace Delegated Authority"}
When splitting an architecture task across multiple AI systems and tools,
trace each decision right. Which system or tool may halt or advance a routine
transition? Which observation requires escalation, who may grant a waiver, and
which role makes the final decision? Adding more systems and tools must not
make those assignments disappear.
:::

Independent challenge must reach a decision owner before consequential use. A
case from clinical genomics illustrates what can happen when it arrives later.

::: {.callout-field-note title="Challenge After Consequential Use"}
In clinical genomics, published predictors were already being used to allocate
patients in clinical trials. Baggerly and Coombes reconstructed the analyses and
found reversed labels, duplicated and inconsistently labeled samples, and other
simple errors, some of which persisted through published corrections
[@BaggerlyCoombes2009].

Independent challenge needs a defined route to the decision owner before or
during consequential use.
:::

Before a consequential architecture transition, reviewers need a route to the
person or role authorized to decide. That path requires people who understand
the relevant tools. An architect need not write every harness, inspect every
timing path, or run every place-and-route job. The responsible organization
must name experts who understand the relevant abstractions, tool contracts,
evidence limits, and likely failure modes well enough to set acceptance
criteria, challenge anomalous results, judge a waiver, and know when a stronger
check is required.
A program that cannot identify someone who can explain what an automated check
establishes, what it misses, and why it may advance a claim cannot responsibly
delegate authority to that check. That requirement runs into a classic irony of
automation. Delegation removes the routine work that once trained the experts
the delegation still requires [@Bainbridge1983Ironies]. Training and assessment
are therefore part of the implementation problem, as discussed in
@sec-what-a-graduate-can-do.

Responsibility must follow decisions across hardware, software, physical
design, and product constraints. A future architect may not be only a CPU
architect, accelerator architect, compiler specialist, or physical-design
expert. Specialists and automated systems can operate pieces of the stack;
authorized roles compose their decisions by determining which hardware
settings may change, which software contracts remain fixed, which workload
scenarios matter, which checks may block a result, and which deployment
consequences are acceptable.

Leveson's central move in safety engineering was to treat safety as a property
of interactions in a sociotechnical control structure rather than the sum of
reliable components [@Leveson2012EngineeringSaferWorld]. The transfer here is
limited because most architecture studies are not safety cases. Still, a
capable model or a passing tool check cannot establish an architectural claim
on its own. Another architect can evaluate the claim only when the record
connects scope, representations, tools, evidence, challenge, and assigned
decision rights.

@fig-architect-owned-boundary separates work that may be delegated from
responsibilities that must stay assigned. Automated methods may represent state,
generate candidates, evaluate proxies, call tools, critique results, summarize
evidence, preserve provenance, and reject candidates under declared rules.
Those are substantial contributions. They may propose scope, evidence policies,
or escalation paths, but they cannot legitimately assign themselves
organizational authority, grant themselves waivers, or become answerable for
product risk. An automated optimizer might tune core parameters against a
thermal objective, for instance, while a package or
product role remains responsible for the rule that blocks a reliability or
certification violation and for any waiver that permits the design to advance.

![**Delegated work and assigned responsibility.** Within a defined study, AI systems, tools, and specialists may assist formulation, exploration, implementation, evaluation, explanation, and defense. Named roles remain responsible for scope, abstraction, evidence standards, automation authority, escalation, final approval, and reopening.](images/F11-architect-owned-boundary){#fig-architect-owned-boundary width="100%" fig-alt="Architecture study containing the six architecture capabilities, delegated assistance, an architectural claim for review, supporting instruments, and a full-width band of architect responsibilities."}

The upper band in @fig-architect-owned-boundary shows where work may be
delegated. AI systems, tools, and domain specialists may assist any of the six
capabilities, and those capabilities need not proceed in a fixed sequence. The
work converges on an architectural claim with a stated proposition, scope,
evidence, counterevidence, and qualification. That claim, rather than the
volume of generated artifacts, is what another architect reviews.

The middle row contains supporting instruments. Design loops organize
iteration, evidence records preserve provenance and uncertainty, cards index
the work, and checks or replay packages help test it. None of those instruments
owns the decision. The full-width band at the bottom therefore remains visible
across every capability. Named roles still own scope, abstraction, evidence
standards, automation authority, escalation, waivers, final approval, and
reopening conditions.

AI can increase the speed and volume of work in the upper band without changing
the obligations in the lower band. An architect therefore has to link the
delegated work to a reviewable claim and the claim to assigned responsibility.
The decision rights in @tbl-decision-rights-matrix apply across the work, not
to a single capability. @tbl-architect-responsibilities translates the lower
band into the records an architect or authorized signoff role must be able to
inspect. Semiconductor programs already use signoff checklists for artifacts.
The table also covers the earlier decisions about scope, evidence, automation,
waivers, and final approval that make those artifacts meaningful.

| **Architect responsibility** | **Why assignment matters** | **What may be automated or delegated** | **Required record** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Intent, scope, and constraints** | The architecture work must serve a real objective rather than an available benchmark or proxy. | Elicit constraints, compare formulations, run policy checks, and reject declared violations. | Problem statement, non-goals, workload slice, hard constraints, and responsible role. |
| **Abstraction and representation** | Encoded state determines what an AI method or reviewer can see, optimize, ignore, or simplify incorrectly. | Translate artifacts, organize traces, find gaps, and propose structured schemas. | Architecture schema, exposed design space, assumptions, hidden-state list, and redaction boundary. |
| **Evidence sufficiency** | Evidence must match the consequence, reversibility, and cost of being wrong. | Gather measurements, track provenance, estimate uncertainty, replay baselines, and route anomalies. | Tool and environment contracts, calibration records, evidence summary, validity scope, and escalation threshold. |
| **Authority, escalation, and waivers** | AI-assisted work needs declared rules for who or what may stop, advance, reopen, or override a decision. | Apply validated checks, reject invalid candidates, advance routine transitions, and notify reviewers. | Authority matrix, automated-check configuration, exception record, waiver rationale, expiration, and reviewer. |
| **Final decision and answerability** | Product, tapeout, deployment, safety, security, cost, and lifecycle consequences need an answerable owner. | Summarize tradeoffs, check completeness, monitor drift, and trigger rollback or revalidation. | Decision note, accepted and rejected alternatives, residual risks, authorized transition, ownership transfer, and reopening condition. |

: **Responsibilities and required records.** Execution may be automated or delegated, while scope, authority, evidence sufficiency, escalation, waivers, final approval, and accountability remain assigned. {#tbl-architect-responsibilities tbl-colwidths="[18,26,27,29]"}

Some studies may use agentic systems[^fn-ml-agentic-system], which can act on
represented study state, call tools, revise plans, use feedback, and coordinate
method roles. When work iterates, they may participate inside a design loop.
Their ability to act does not assign them organizational responsibility. As
these systems become more capable, the authority
delegated to them and the responsible roles around them must be stated more
precisely.

[^fn-ml-agentic-system]: An AI system capable of pursuing complex goals over time, making its own decisions about which tools to use and what steps to take next. Here, such systems participate inside architecture studies with stated authority limits. Permission to act does not make them responsible for organizational decisions.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="Trace the Decision"}
If an AI system proposes an invalid artifact during a study, the review record
must show who owned each responsibility. Can you trace which person or
organizational role defined the scope, authorized automated checks, set the
evidence threshold, approved any waiver, and made the final decision for the
authorized transition? If those assignments disappear, the architecture
decision is no longer traceable.
:::

## Making Tacit Knowledge Reviewable

Architecture constraints do not all begin in a specification. Engineers also
rely on tacit knowledge, which Polanyi distinguished from knowledge that can be
fully stated [@Polanyi1966TacitDimension]. A packaging engineer may know that a
particular chiplet partition will break the power-delivery mesh. A compiler
lead may know that the current LLVM backend cannot lower a proposed matrix
layout. A senior architect may anticipate that an accelerator's coherency
traffic will starve the XR display controller. A local proxy may miss all three
integration failures.

Domain experts already recover this context before consequential reviews. They
surface undocumented constraints, examine tool assumptions and prior waivers,
resolve conflicts, and decide which judgments apply to the current workload,
software stack, and implementation target. AI can help search design reviews,
waiver histories, and tool reports or interview experts. It can then propose
constraints and missing cases for review. A named compiler, interface,
physical-design, or product owner must decide what enters the architecture
record.

The distinction matters because an AI system can act only on the
state it can observe and the interfaces it may use. If the recorded state omits
a packaging limit, unsupported lowering path, coherency constraint, or display
deadline, the method may optimize an invalid candidate. Converting a validated
judgment into an environment constraint or rejection check can stop that
candidate before it consumes expensive simulation or implementation time.

Each entry also needs a source, scope, responsible owner, and revalidation
condition. A constraint tied to one LLVM revision, process design kit, workload
mix, or approved waiver cannot silently become a permanent rule. Conflicting
domain advice, a tool upgrade, an expired waiver, or new physical evidence must
return the entry to a named expert. @fig-tacit-extraction-bridge shows where AI
can help and where that authority boundary remains.

![**Reviewing tacit knowledge before using it.** AI can help elicit and organize domain knowledge, but a named domain expert must validate constraints, assumptions, and rejection conditions. Conflicts, expiration, or new evidence require another review.](images/F10-tacit-extraction-bridge){#fig-tacit-extraction-bridge width="100%" fig-alt="Diagram showing domain knowledge passing through AI-assisted elicitation and domain validation before entering represented study state, with conflict, expiry, and new evidence returning the record for revalidation."}

The figure begins with situated knowledge about compiler and ISA constraints,
physical implementation limits, and workload or product obligations. AI-assisted
elicitation can organize that material and flag contradictions, but the blocked
path shows that it cannot write directly into authoritative state. A named
domain expert challenges each proposed entry with tools and prior records, then
approves, rejects, or qualifies it.

Only validated constraints, assumptions, rejection checks, and validity
conditions enter the represented state. The return arrow matters as much as
the forward path. Conflict, expiration, or new evidence sends an entry back for
review. AI can make unstated architecture knowledge easier to find and inspect.
The person answerable for the architecture decision need not perform every
specialist task, but must know which assumptions can change the decision, who
validated them, and when they need another review.

## The Strongest Objections

Four objections define important limits on the argument.

The first objection is that Architecture 2.0 is established experimental
methodology under new names. Provenance, baselines, rejection criteria, and
held-out validation are not new, and the objection is correct about that
lineage. Machine learning first entered computer architecture inside mechanisms
and human-specified methods [@ChenPenneyJimenez2020AIArchitecture]. Structured
evidence from assurance cases and GSN [@Kelly2004GSN], automotive functional
safety under ISO 26262 [@ISO2018Iso26262], and multi-fidelity modeling
[@PeherstorferWillcoxGunzburger2018Multifidelity] also predate this material.
The added architecture problem appears when AI helps formulate the question,
create alternatives, select tool actions, interpret results, or produce the
claim itself. Those AI-produced alternatives, tool choices, interpretations,
and claims must remain inspectable, along with the authority assigned to each
step. Established methods supply part of the answer; they do not remove the new
review burden.

The second objection is that AI for systems design is overhyped and may add no
value to a particular study. That is sometimes true. The matched run in
@sec-running-the-loop finds no AI-arm advantage and rejects the proposed
mechanism. Any method that requires an AI win would be marketing. A null result,
a simpler baseline, and a decision to stop are legitimate outcomes.

The third objection is that the design-loop card adds process overhead that
will not survive a deadline. The card is an optional compact index for studies
where tools or AI systems can advance candidates faster than
reviewers can reconstruct the evidence. A short initial pass can reveal whether
a result rests on a proxy nobody validated or a baseline nobody documented.
Skipping the check may move discovery of a weak proxy or undocumented baseline
to a later and more expensive stage. A responsible reviewer should compare the
time spent maintaining the card with the reconstruction work and defects it
actually prevents. A simple, human-run comparison may need only a concise run
record; the card is justified when delegation makes the evidence path hard to
reconstruct.

The fourth objection is that assigned final decision rights are too
conservative for more capable AI systems. Validated checks can reject
candidates, routine transitions can advance automatically, and specialized
reviews can be delegated. Delegation can expand when evidence shows that those
checks work within a stated boundary. A reliability waiver, tapeout approval,
or product transition still needs a responsible role because technical
competence does not confer organizational authority. This distinction does not
depend on a permanent weakness in current models.

Not every architecture problem needs AI, a design loop, or a card. When AI does
help produce or evaluate an
architectural claim, another architect must still be able to inspect the claim,
its evidence, and the authority behind the resulting decision.

## What the Field Must Maintain

An architect cannot remain answerable for a decision if the evidence, tool
versions, challenge path, and ownership record disappear after review. One
well-documented study is useful; learning across studies requires shared
infrastructure that someone maintains. The individual resources in
@sec-appendix-c-architecture-20-resources will change, but three requirements
remain:

- **Concrete study records:** Versioned tasks, workload packets, environment
  contracts, matched conventional methods, evidence labeled by source,
  fidelity, and scope, failures, and disclosure boundaries make comparisons
  interpretable.
- **Credible challenge paths:** Held-out cases, cross-tool checks, qualified
  independent evaluation, conflict rules, waivers, appeals, and retest paths
  give a disputed claim somewhere to go.
- **Lifecycle ownership:** Named maintainers, version policies, archived release
  snapshots, drift checks, revalidation, and retirement rules keep a resource
  from silently changing underneath a claim.

Together, those requirements support three ways to scrutinize the work. Public
comparison requires enough shared information to determine whether tasks,
baselines, tool paths, budgets, and outcomes are comparable. Authorized
inspection allows a qualified reviewer to examine protected evidence that
cannot be released, accompanied by a public account of what the reviewer
inspected and what conclusion it supports. Replay requires runnable inputs, an
environment, provenance, and the right to execute them. Public reporting,
protected review, and replay may overlap, but none should stand in for another.

An undifferentiated collection of model output and tool logs is not sufficient.
A reusable record connects a question and baseline to the represented state,
candidate actions, tool observations, failed alternatives, interpretation, and
decision. Logs can provide observations and provenance, but an architect must
supply the architecture question, explain what a failed check means, and bound
the claim.

Data without documented tool interfaces are ambiguous. Harnesses without
held-out evaluation can mistake setup differences for method improvements.
Reporting formats without independent review standardize paperwork rather than
evidence. Repositories preserve artifacts, while maintainers define versions,
comparison rules, conflict procedures, appeals, and retest policies. Standards
should follow repeated useful practice rather than freeze today's models.

Shared infrastructure does not require raw proprietary design disclosure.
Public reporting can begin with records that state the task, versions, exposed
interfaces, accepted and rejected candidates, available evidence, redactions,
and named roles. Those records let reviewers ask not only what result was
reported, but which comparison produced it, what its scope was, and which parts
are open to inspection.

One difficult case is failure knowledge from proprietary physical-design
flows. If those records remain entirely private, outsiders cannot inspect the
evidence and future systems repeatedly encounter the same dead ends. A useful
exchange format would separate protected implementation details from a failure
record with a stated scope, without pretending that the public proxy reproduces
the private result.

One possible approach is a redacted failure record linked to an open or
synthetic proxy. When an industrial AI-assisted study rejects a candidate
because of a proprietary physical-design constraint, the study authors might
map the failure to a public task that preserves the rejection lesson without
exposing the design rule. The public record could report the failure relative
to the mapped task when the mapping preserves the rejection condition. It must
not imply that the public proxy is equivalent to the proprietary
physical-design result.

The example in @lst-redacted-trace shows how authors can record the boundary of
an architectural exploration while redacting absolute
dimensions and proprietary design rules. It is a valid schema 2.0 design-loop
card rather than a separate redaction schema. Its context and disclosure
profiles are complete, while inspectability, independent review, and
decision-right profiles are marked partial and replay is not claimed. The
public record names what was attempted and rejected, but another group cannot
audit or replay the protected evidence. It can document scope and omissions,
but it cannot support a public comparison.

The excerpt below shows the opening fields; the complete validated card ships
with the companion examples and continues with the claim, evidence, failed
check, decision rights, and recorded decision.

```{.yaml #lst-redacted-trace include="../examples/design-loop-cards/redacted-context-v2.yaml" startLine="1" endLine="26" lst-cap="**Redacted Record Excerpt:** The opening fields of a schema-valid v2 card state its public profile gaps, protected context, constraints, and non-claims."}
```

Before using a redacted record for comparison, check that it still states the
task and workload class, action-observation interface, material tool and model
versions, feedback budget, accepted and rejected candidates, evidence status,
redaction boundary, and decision owner. If any comparison-defining field is
hidden, the record can provide context but cannot support a public ranking.

Names and popularity say little about whether a benchmark, dataset,
environment, or method fits an architecture question. The versioned registry in
@sec-appendix-c-architecture-20-resources maintains those resources. Judge each
one by whether it helps a study show its question, AI role, state, tool path,
failures, evidence, disclosure boundary, and named decision owner.

Some disputes cannot be settled from the public record. Released artifacts may
permit only a partial comparison while the strongest evidence remains
proprietary. A redacted record can show exactly where public inspection ends,
while a named protected reviewer may be able to examine more. Neither
arrangement creates replay when the runnable design and training context remain
unavailable. The recurring AlphaChip case illustrates this limit.

::: {.callout-war-story title="The public record still could not settle the flagship result"}
**The claim.** The original AlphaChip work reported expert-quality macro
placement and later deployment [@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement].

**The gap.** Independent assessments disputed the baselines, proxy, compute comparison,
and reproducibility. Because the strongest results depended on proprietary
designs and training context, no outside group could recreate the full
evaluation [@ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL; @GoldieEtAl2024ThatChipHasSailed].

**The lesson.** The public record does not settle the technical dispute. The
practical goal is to make the next one easier to investigate. Shared cases and tools support
public comparison; redacted records state what remains private; and named
reviewers inspect protected evidence. These measures do not replace missing
evidence, but they show where independent verification ends.
:::

The same infrastructure should preserve failure records, including failed
runs, rejected candidates, invalid configurations, stale benchmarks, bad
proxies, tool errors, and ideas ruled out by expert judgment. Future AI systems
need those records to avoid rediscovering known dead ends. A community that
records only successful artifacts gives both people and AI systems a distorted
view of architecture practice.

No organization is expected to publish every internal trace. The field can
instead build schemas, examples, synthetic tasks, open benchmarks, redacted
records, and review artifacts that researchers can inspect and compare.
Organizations are more likely to preserve useful failure records when doing so
prevents repeated high-fidelity failures, makes handoffs cheaper, supports
review credit, and shows when an old rejection should be retired rather than
repeated forever.

### Start With One Well-Documented Study

An architect need not wait for shared infrastructure across the whole stack.
The minimum useful target is one well-documented study attached to a design
review, design-space exploration, or paper. Its record should satisfy five
checks:

- **Decision scope:** Name the task and the decision it can inform.
- **Tool path:** Connect at least one tool path to a versioned environment
  contract.
- **Run record:** Preserve successful and failed runs.
- **Evidence boundary:** State what evidence supports the survivor.
- **Decision rights:** Assign automated and human decision rights.

If a second person can reconstruct what was tried, what failed, what advanced
automatically, what required review, and who remains answerable, the record is
already useful. @sec-appendix-a-bootstrapping provides a
step-by-step bootstrap path, with five post-run checks on the execution record.

The record owner should also define its lifecycle. A shared failure record
needs reuse limits, revalidation rules, and a retirement path so a local
rejection does not become a false universal rule. Tacit knowledge belongs only
where it can be named as state, rationale, waiver, or owner judgment.
Model-side compute and energy cost, post-silicon telemetry, integrity records,
and legal, IP, or export-control boundaries belong whenever they affect the
claim. These fields determine whether the record remains valid when tools,
workloads, models, and owners change.

These records matter only if architects know how to use and challenge them.
Education and assessment should test that ability directly.

## What a Graduate Can Do {#sec-what-a-graduate-can-do}

An Architecture 2.0 curriculum succeeds only if learners can use these ideas on
a new architecture problem. Teaching and assessment should therefore align
with the work graduates must actually perform
[@Biggs1996ConstructiveAlignment]. The central test brings the six capabilities
together. A graduate should be able to decide where AI assistance is useful,
conduct a bounded study, and defend an evidence-limited recommendation that
another architect can challenge. Each capability leaves observable evidence,
from a study brief for formulation to a defended decision memo for the final
recommendation (@tbl-graduate-capabilities).

| **Capability** | **A graduate can** | **Performance evidence** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Formulate** | Bound an unfamiliar architecture problem with a baseline, alternatives, constraints, claim, and non-goals. | Study brief. |
| **Explore** | Choose AI or conventional methods, compare meaningful alternatives, and preserve why serious candidates failed or were rejected. | Alternative set, method rationale, and rejected-candidate record. |
| **Implement** | Produce enough executable structure and tool connectivity to test the claim. | Checkable artifact and tool contract. |
| **Evaluate** | Use representative workloads, matched architecture baselines, justified fidelity, uncertainty, counterevidence, and total feedback cost, plus a conventional or no-AI comparator when benefit from AI is claimed. | Evaluation packet. |
| **Explain** | Form and test a mechanism-level account of why the result occurred. | Mechanism hypothesis plus sensitivity, intervention, or ablation. |
| **Defend** | Make a bounded recommendation with tradeoffs, limits, ownership, and reversal conditions. | Decision memo and oral or written defense. |

: **Observable evidence for the six capabilities.** A learner demonstrates Architecture 2.0 through a bounded architecture study and defensible decision, not through terminology alone. {#tbl-graduate-capabilities tbl-colwidths="[16,48,28]"}

The listed artifacts are evidence of the work, not a completion checklist. They
should be judged together. A justified finding that AI assistance added no
value can satisfy the evaluation outcome; the learner is not required to
manufacture an AI win. The capstone should use a problem other than the
lighthouse case. The learner receives a new,
tractable architecture objective within their domain competence, together with
a feasible tool and evidence budget, and must bound the study. The final record
includes a study brief, alternative and rejection record, method rationale,
checkable artifact and tool path, evaluation packet, tested mechanism
explanation, and a decision memo with stated limits that the learner defends under
independent challenge. @sec-running-the-loop supplies one worked example of an
iterative study turn, and the companion labs provide selected practice. Because
the problem is new, the learner must carry all six capabilities beyond the
examples used in the book.

The same outcomes can guide review of work by learners, researchers, and
practitioners. In a live project, the evidence from each capability helps the
reviewer decide whether the work can advance.

### A Practitioner Path

Classic computer architecture already relies on quantitative metrics and
disciplined comparison [@HennessyPatterson2017QuantitativeApproach]. The six
capabilities fit that familiar review work. Seven review checkpoints make that
connection concrete:

1. **Frame the question.** State the architecture question, comparator, claims,
   non-claims, constraints, and decision the evidence may inform.
2. **Identify the limiting resource.** Determine whether representation, allowed
   actions, feedback, review capacity, or coordination is slowing the work.
3. **Prepare the inputs and tools.** Version the context, environment, tools, allowed
   actions, observations, failures, budgets, and decision rights.
4. **Compare methods fairly.** Match AI and conventional arms on workload,
   tools, resource assumptions, and evaluation opportunity.
5. **Execute and record.** Preserve every material input, output, failed run,
   rejected alternative, amendment, and integrity check.
6. **Evaluate separate claims.** Judge the architecture outcome, AI contribution,
   and mechanism account independently; stop at what the evidence supports.
7. **Approve the action and define when to revisit it.** Record who recommended
   the action, who could reject it, who approved any waiver, who made the final
   decision, the residual risk, and what observation would reverse the decision.

The checkpoints do not prescribe an order. They identify points at which a
reviewer needs something concrete to inspect. A project may stop when a
comparison is invalid, a tool path fails, or the evidence does not support the
proposed action. A design-loop card can index the work, while the architecture
question, artifacts, evidence, explanation, and decision remain the substance.

## Research Agenda

Today's model feature lists will age. The responsibilities in this chapter lead
to three harder questions that will remain open.

1. **When does AI add architectural value?** Under matched tool, human, compute,
   and feedback budgets, how can architects separate improvements in artifact
   quality, architectural insight, and decision quality?
2. **What transfers, and when should evidence expire?** Which representations,
   environments, proxies, mechanism explanations, and failure records remain
   valid across workloads, tools, abstraction levels, and technologies? What
   change should trigger revalidation when a prior result would reverse a
   ranking or invalidate a claim?
3. **Which challenge and authority assignments improve decisions?** How should
   automated checks, independent reviewers, protected evaluators, waiver
   approvers, and decision owners be arranged to catch errors without making
   review the limiting resource? The answer must account for false accepts,
   false rejects, escalation, overreliance, and review cost.

Self-improving design systems make all three questions harder. A future system
may revise its architecture representation, repair a tool interface, choose a
new proxy, or reorganize failure records. The same changes can hide failures,
overfit a benchmark, or weaken an evidence check. Each change therefore needs a
stated validation path and an authorized role that decides whether prior
evidence still applies.

The target can also move after a decision. Continuously regenerated software
and changing fleet workloads can age a hardware or software artifact, as
@sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales and @sec-loop-patterns-across-stack showed.
Earlier Architecture 2.0 work described the horizon as increasing autonomy
balanced with human expertise [@ReddiYazdanbakhsh2025Architecture20]. The open
question is how to revise validation and authority as targets change without
carrying stale evidence or stale approvals into the next decision.

## Final Thoughts

Across all six capabilities, a reviewer should be able to find the claim, the
authority delegated to AI systems and tools, the evidence limit, and the owner
of any consequential decision. The architect is responsible for making and
maintaining those assignments. Routine checks need not wait for manual
approval, and one person need not hold every decision right. The record must
still show who or what may act, when an exception escalates, what evidence
supports the proposed action, and who remains answerable when the context
changes.

::: {#pri-human-decision-owner .callout-design-principle title="Name the decision owner"}
Routine checks may be automated or delegated, but consequential decisions
still need a named owner. Record who may advance, reject, escalate, or waive a
result; who has final authority; and who must revisit the decision when the
evidence or context changes.
:::

Assigned decision ownership becomes reviewable through three concrete tests.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="Test the Assignment"}
- **Assignment test:** Can a reviewer identify the role that set scope, authorized automated checks, judged evidence sufficiency, and made the final decision?
- **Exception test:** Are escalation, waiver, rollback, and reopening paths specified before an exception occurs?
- **Lifecycle test:** When tools, workloads, or owners change, does the responsible organization transfer or retire those assignments rather than silently preserving stale authority?
:::

The lighthouse prompt in @sec-moonshot may look like a request to replace a
computer architect with a system that produces an XR subsystem from a sentence.
Even if AI eventually produces most of the artifacts, the architect or another
authorized architecture role must understand which workload, software, ISA,
and physical assumptions shaped the result. That role must decide whether the
tool evidence, rejected alternatives, and unresolved waivers support the
proposed action. It remains answerable for that action.

The executed study in @sec-running-the-loop demonstrates a narrower present
standard. It includes one real model interaction, a matched conventional arm,
complete tool results, a null AI-contribution finding, a falsified mechanism
account, network-free replay, and a decision that remains pending until the
responsible person confirms it. The value lies in what another architect can
inspect and decide, not in claiming an AI win.

Architecture 2.0 is the engineering discipline of using AI, grounded in
architectural representations, tools, and experiments, to formulate, explore,
implement, evaluate, explain, and defend computer architecture decisions.
Under that definition, AI may help produce the evidence and argument. An
authorized architecture role must still understand what the resulting evidence
establishes, decide what action it justifies, and remain answerable for the
result.
